quiz.title: Unit 5 Quiz 12, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Comprehensive Formula Writing
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Writing Chemical Formulas for Ionic Compounds, Covalent Compounds, Acids, Polyatomic Ions, Transition Metals, Stock System

questions.question: What is the formula for sodium bicarbonate?
questions.explanation: Sodium bicarbonate is $\text{NaHCO}_3$. It contains sodium ($\text{Na}^+$) and the hydrogen carbonate ion ($\text{HCO}_3^-$), commonly known as bicarbonate.

questions.question: What is the formula for ammonium nitrate?
questions.explanation: Ammonium nitrate is $\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3$. It contains ammonium ($\text{NH}_4^+$) and nitrate ($\text{NO}_3^-$), both polyatomic ions with 1+ and 1- charges respectively.

questions.question: What is the formula for copper(II) sulfate?
questions.explanation: Copper(II) sulfate is $\text{CuSO}_4$. Copper(II) has a +2 charge ($\text{Cu}^{2+}$), and sulfate is $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ with a -2 charge, so they combine in a 1:1 ratio.

questions.question: What is the formula for iron(II) sulfate?
questions.explanation: Iron(II) sulfate is $\text{FeSO}_4$. Iron(II) has a +2 charge ($\text{Fe}^{2+}$), and sulfate is $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ with a -2 charge, so they combine in a 1:1 ratio.

questions.question: What is the formula for potassium hydroxide?
questions.explanation: Potassium hydroxide is $\text{KOH}$. Potassium has a +1 charge ($\text{K}^+$), and hydroxide is $\text{OH}^-$ with a -1 charge, so they combine in a 1:1 ratio.

questions.question: What is the formula for silver nitrate?
questions.explanation: Silver nitrate is $\text{AgNO}_3$. Silver typically forms $\text{Ag}^+$ with a +1 charge, and nitrate is $\text{NO}_3^-$ with a -1 charge, so they combine in a 1:1 ratio.

questions.question: What is the formula for lead(IV) oxide?
questions.explanation: Lead(IV) oxide is $\text{PbO}_2$. Lead(IV) has a +4 charge ($\text{Pb}^{4+}$), and oxide is $\text{O}^{2-}$ with a -2 charge, so two oxides are needed. $\text{PbO}$ (A) would be lead(II) oxide.

questions.question: What is the formula for tin(II) sulfide?
questions.explanation: Tin(II) sulfide is $\text{SnS}$. Tin(II) has a +2 charge ($\text{Sn}^{2+}$), and sulfide is $\text{S}^{2-}$ with a -2 charge, so they combine in a 1:1 ratio.

questions.question: What is the formula for calcium hydroxide?
questions.explanation: Calcium hydroxide is $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$. Calcium has a +2 charge ($\text{Ca}^{2+}$), and hydroxide is $\text{OH}^-$ with a -1 charge, so two hydroxide ions are needed.

questions.question: What is the formula for magnesium nitride?
questions.explanation: Magnesium nitride is $\text{Mg}_3\text{N}_2$. Magnesium has a +2 charge ($\text{Mg}^{2+}$), and nitride is $\text{N}^{3-}$ with a -3 charge. To balance charges, three $\text{Mg}^{2+}$ (+6) combine with two $\text{N}^{3-}$ (-6).

questions.question: What is the formula for potassium permanganate?
questions.explanation: Potassium permanganate is $\text{KMnO}_4$. Potassium has a +1 charge ($\text{K}^+$), and permanganate is $\text{MnO}_4^-$ with a -1 charge, so they combine in a 1:1 ratio. Note: Options A and B are identical in the original, but the correct formula is $\text{KMnO}_4$.

questions.question: What is the formula for zinc nitrate?
questions.explanation: Zinc nitrate is $\text{Zn(NO}_3)_2$. Zinc typically forms $\text{Zn}^{2+}$ with a +2 charge, and nitrate is $\text{NO}_3^-$ with a -1 charge, so two nitrates are needed.

questions.question: What is the formula for sulfur hexafluoride?
questions.explanation: Sulfur hexafluoride is $\text{SF}_6$. It is a covalent compound where sulfur forms six single bonds with six fluorine atoms. The prefix 'hexa-' indicates six fluorine atoms.

questions.question: What is the formula for diphosphorus pentoxide?
questions.explanation: Diphosphorus pentoxide is $\text{P}_2\text{O}_5$. The prefix 'di-' indicates two phosphorus atoms, and 'penta-' indicates five oxygen atoms.

questions.question: What is the formula for phosphoric acid?
questions.explanation: Phosphoric acid is $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$. It contains the phosphate ion ($\text{PO}_4^{3-}$) combined with three hydrogen ions. $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_3$ (C) would be phosphorous acid.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered formula writing for a wide variety of compounds! 🧪📝
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review compounds with polyatomic ions and transition metals.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on charge balancing and common ion charges.
scoreMessages.0: Please review how to write formulas for ionic compounds, covalent compounds, and acids.
